stories.headline: AI safety staffing gaps raise concerns as companies rush to build smarter systems
stories.summary: AI companies claim to prioritize safety for humanity's benefit, but staffing data reveals a stark imbalance with only a few hundred personnel dedicated to making systems safe. This gap is particularly troubling as AI adoption expands to military use like the Pentagon and the race for more advanced AI intensifies.
stories.original_title: Do AI companies care about safety as much as they claim to? Their staff allocation data suggests not
stories.context: Artificial intelligence, or AI, is computer software that can learn and make decisions somewhat like a human brain. For years, experts have warned that as AI becomes more powerful, we need people专门 focused on making sure these systems don't accidentally cause harm or make dangerous mistakes. This worry grew more urgent recently when AI advanced so quickly that it can now write essays, create images, and even help with military planning. What's concerning is that while tech companies are racing to build ever-smarter AI to beat their competitors, they're hiring thousands of engineers to make AI more powerful but only putting a few hundred people on safety teams. This is like building faster and faster cars while hiring almost no safety engineers to test the brakes.
stories.why_it_matters: AI systems are getting smarter and more powerful every day, but the companies making them aren't hiring enough people to check if these systems are safe. This is dangerous because powerful AI is being used by the military and in important areas where mistakes could hurt people.
stories.other_side: Some argue that companies need to focus on building better AI first, and safety checks slow down progress that could help solve real-world problems. They believe the market will naturally push companies to be safe if their products cause problems.

stories.headline: Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 revealed with 5,000mAh battery breakthrough
stories.summary: Samsung's upcoming Galaxy Z Fold 8 is set to revolutionize the foldable smartphone market with significant improvements in design, battery performance, and usability. The device addresses limitations of its predecessor with a substantial 5,000mAh battery, aiming to strengthen Samsung's position in the competitive foldable segment.
stories.original_title: Samsung Finally Did It: The Galaxy Z Fold 8's 5,000mAh Battery Revealed
stories.context: For the past decade, smartphone screens have been stuck in one shape—flat rectangles that can't change size. However, engineers recently figured out how to make screens that can actually fold in half like a piece of paper, opening up new possibilities for devices that can transform from a phone into a small tablet. The first generations of these foldable phones had major problems, especially with battery life because the batteries had to be split into two smaller pieces to fit in the folding mechanism. A 5,000mAh battery (a measurement of how much power a battery can hold) is considered large for a regular smartphone, but achieving this size in a foldable phone has been extremely difficult until now due to space constraints. Samsung's breakthrough means foldable phones can finally match the battery life of traditional phones, making them much more practical for everyday use.
stories.why_it_matters: Foldable phones have always struggled with running out of battery too quickly because they need power for two screens. If Samsung finally solved this problem with a bigger battery, foldable phones could become just as practical as regular phones for everyone.
stories.other_side: Critics point out that foldable phones are still really expensive and the folding parts can break more easily than normal phones. They say better battery won't matter if people can't afford to buy them or if they're worried about durability.

stories.headline: Nvidia and Uber partner on end-to-end self-driving system for robotaxis
stories.summary: Nvidia unveiled a complete autonomous driving technology stack at GTC 2026 and announced it will deploy the full solution through ride-hailing platform Uber. This partnership marks a significant turning point in the robotaxi race, intensifying competition among companies developing fully autonomous vehicle technology.
stories.original_title: Nvidia and Uber team up on end-to-end self-driving system for robotaxis
stories.context: Self-driving cars, also called autonomous vehicles, use cameras, radar, and AI software to navigate roads without human drivers. For over a decade, companies like Google's Wayno and Tesla have been racing to build robotaxis—taxis that can pick up passengers and drive themselves with no one behind the wheel. However, building a completely safe self-driving system is incredibly complex because the car needs to handle every possible road situation, from traffic lights to pedestrians to bad weather. Nvidia is a company that makes the specialized computer chips and software that power AI, but until now they've just been selling these parts to car companies rather than building the whole system themselves. By partnering with Uber, which already has millions of riders and a massive network of drivers, Nvidia can now test and deploy their complete self-driving technology in real cities much faster than if they had to build their own ride service from scratch.
stories.why_it_matters: Self-driving robotaxis could change how we get around cities by making rides cheaper and available 24/7 without human drivers. This partnership between Nvidia and Uber means we might see driverless cars on real streets much sooner than expected.
stories.other_side: Many people worry that self-driving technology isn't ready yet and could cause accidents or get confused in tricky situations. There are also concerns about what happens to all the human drivers who would lose their jobs.

stories.headline: British Airways becomes first UK airline to offer Starlink internet
stories.summary: British Airways has partnered with Elon Musk's SpaceX to become the first UK airline offering Starlink satellite internet service to passengers. The deal brings high-speed, low-latency internet connectivity to flights, potentially transforming the in-flight experience and setting a new standard for airline connectivity.
stories.original_title: BA first UK airline to offer Musk's Starlink internet
stories.context: For decades, airplane internet has been notoriously slow and expensive because it relied on ground-based cell towers or older satellite technology. The problem was that planes fly so high and fast that connecting to the internet was like trying to have a clear phone conversation while running through a tunnel. SpaceX, the rocket company founded by Elon Musk, launched a solution called Starlink—they've sent thousands of small satellites into orbit around Earth to blanket the planet with fast internet coverage from space. Unlike traditional satellites that sit very far away (making signals slow), Starlink satellites orbit much closer to Earth, which means the time it takes for data to travel back and forth (called latency) is much shorter. When British Airways signed up, they became the first UK airline to offer passengers the same kind of fast, responsive internet they have at home rather than the sluggish connections frequent flyers are used to.
stories.why_it_matters: Internet on planes has always been slow, unreliable, or expensive. With high-speed Starlink, passengers could finally work, watch movies, or video chat from 35,000 feet just like at home.
stories.other_side: Some privacy advocates worry that space-based internet companies could track people's online activities more easily. Others point out that even with better technology, airlines might still charge high prices for the service.

stories.headline: Google introduces new security process for Android app sideloading
stories.summary: Google has detailed a new one-time security process that will hide certain app sideloading options on Android devices. The more restrictive policy requires developers to be verified to distribute software, marking a significant shift in how users can install applications from unverified sources.
stories.original_title: Android will hide some app sideloading behind a new one-time security process
stories.context: When you buy a phone, it comes with an official app store like the Google Play Store where you can safely download apps that have been checked and approved. However, some people want to install apps from other places—this is called sideloading, and it's like installing software on your computer from a website instead of a store. For years, Android has been relatively open about this, letting users download and install apps from almost anywhere, which gives people freedom but also opens the door to malicious apps that could steal data or harm the phone. What changed recently is that bad actors began abusing this openness more frequently, creating fake apps and harmful software that trick users into installing them. Google's new policy means both the people making apps and the apps themselves need to be verified with Google before users can easily sideload them—like requiring an ID check before someone can sell you something from a street vendor instead of a licensed store.
stories.why_it_matters: Currently, anyone can install almost any app on an Android phone from the internet, which is flexible but dangerous because bad apps can steal your data. Google's new rule will make it harder for criminals to spread harmful apps, making phones safer for regular users.
stories.other_side: People who value choice and freedom say Google should let users decide what to install on their own devices. Critics worry this gives too much control to Google and could block useful apps they simply don't like.

stories.headline: VC Montis raises €50M for European energy and industrial tech startups
stories.summary: Warsaw-based venture capital firm Montis Capital has secured €50M from the European Investment Fund and Polish Development Fund to invest in 20-25 early-stage companies. The fund will target startups working on energy transition, industrial automation, and artificial intelligence across Europe.
stories.original_title: VC Montis raises €50M to back Europe's energy and industrial tech startups
stories.context: Venture capital firms are investment companies that give money to new businesses (startups) in exchange for owning a piece of those companies, hoping the startups become huge successes. Most VC money historically flowed to places like Silicon Valley in California, and relatively little went to European startups, creating what was essentially a funding gap that held back innovation in Europe. Meanwhile, the world is facing two major challenges: countries need to transition away from fossil fuels to cleaner energy sources, and factories need to modernize using automation and AI to remain competitive. Montis Capital, based in Poland, was created specifically to invest in European companies solving these problems, and by securing €50 million (about $54 million) from major European funding institutions, they now have the resources to help 20-25 promising startups get off the ground.
stories.why_it_matters: Europe needs new technology companies focused on clean energy and better factories if it wants to fight climate change and compete globally. This money helps startup founders get their ideas off the ground instead of moving to other countries.
stories.other_side: Some experts worry that government-backed investment funds might pick the wrong companies based on politics rather than good business ideas. They also point out that €50 million split across 25 startups might not be enough money to make a real difference.

stories.headline: DxO PhotoLab 9.6 expands DeepPRIME XD3 support to all cameras
stories.summary: DxO has released PhotoLab 9.6, bringing industry-leading DeepPRIME XD3 noise reduction technology to every camera model, along with new storage solutions. The update makes what is considered the best noise reduction engine in photography accessible to a broader range of photographers and camera systems.
stories.original_title: DxO PhotoLab 9.6: DeepPRIME XD3 Support for Every Camera and Storage Solutions
stories.context: Digital photography has a persistent problem called noise—when you take photos in low light or with older cameras, the images often look grainy or speckled, kind of like looking through a dirty window. For years, software companies have tried different ways to remove this noise through computer processing, but traditional methods either didn't work well or made photos look fake or blurry, like someone used too much smoothing in a photo editing app. DeepPRIME XD3 is DxO's advanced noise reduction technology that uses artificial intelligence to study millions of photos and learn the difference between real image detail and unwanted noise, then intelligently remove only the noise while keeping the picture sharp. Until now, this impressive technology only worked with certain camera models, but with PhotoLab 9.6, DxO has made it available for every camera—meaning photographers using any brand or model of camera can now get the same dramatically improved low-light photos, not just those with the newest, most expensive equipment.
stories.why_it_matters: Photos taken in the dark often look grainy or noisy, making them hard to use or share. This technology now works on every camera, meaning anyone can take clear photos even in bad lighting conditions without buying expensive new equipment.
stories.other_side: Professional photographers say that heavy computer processing can sometimes make photos look artificial or weird compared to keeping the original image. Some also argue that learning to take better photos manually is still more important than relying on software fixes.

stories.headline: AMD releases Adrenalin Edition 26.3.1 WHQL drivers with game support
stories.summary: AMD has launched new WHQL-certified drivers adding support for upcoming titles Crimson Desert and Death Stranding 2, along with FSR Upscaling 4.1 for Radeon RX 9000 series cards. The release also addresses four specific bug fixes including a Cyberpunk 2077 crash issue, improving overall gaming stability and performance.
stories.original_title: AMD Releases Adrenalin Edition 26.3.1 WHQL Drivers
stories.context: When you play video games on a computer, special software called a graphics driver tells the computer's graphics card how to properly display the game—the graphics card is the hardware component responsible for rendering all the visual elements like characters, explosions, and landscapes. Game developers constantly release new games that push graphics cards to their limits, and graphics card manufacturers like AMD need to update their drivers to ensure these new games run smoothly and without crashing. WHQL certification stands for Windows Hardware Quality Labs, which is Microsoft's official testing process that confirms a driver works properly and won't cause problems with Windows—think of it like a safety stamp of approval. The new Crimson Desert and Death Stranding 2 games are upcoming titles that demand more from graphics cards, and FSR Upscaling 4.1 is AMD's technology that helps games look sharper and run faster by using AI to enhance the image quality without requiring as much computing power.
stories.why_it_matters: Video games keep getting more demanding, so graphics card companies need to constantly update their software to make games run smoothly. These new drivers fix crashes and improve picture quality, which is crucial for gamers who want their expensive hardware to actually work properly.
stories.other_side: PC gamers sometimes get frustrated that companies release so many driver updates instead of just making the hardware work perfectly from day one. There's also the risk that new drivers might accidentally break other games or features that previously worked fine.

stories.headline: Greek ports undergo digital transformation with smart applications
stories.summary: Greek ports are embracing digital transformation through smart applications, which provide significant resilience benefits during crisis periods. The initiative faces challenges from state bureaucracy but represents a necessary path toward modernizing port infrastructure and operations.
stories.original_title: 1st Blue Heritage Summit Thessaloniki: The digital transformation of Greek ports
stories.context: For decades, many ports around the world have operated using paperwork, phone calls, and manual processes that haven't changed much since the mid-20th century. When ships arrive, workers must manually track cargo, coordinate between different companies, and handle tons of paperwork—a system that's slow, inefficient, and prone to human error. Digital transformation means replacing these old methods with computer systems, apps, and automated tools that can track everything in real time and share information instantly between all the different organizations involved in running a port. This became especially important after the COVID-19 pandemic caused massive disruptions in global shipping, as ports with better digital systems were able to adapt more quickly and keep operating while others faced severe delays. Greek ports are now trying to modernize, but they face the common challenge of government bureaucracy—layers of rules, approvals, and procedures that can slow down or block new technology from being implemented.
stories.why_it_matters: Ships bring in most of the food and products countries need, so ports need to work efficiently to keep prices low and supplies steady. Making ports "smart" with digital tools helps them handle problems like weather, pandemics, or worker shortages better than old paper-based methods.
stories.other_side: Government workers and longtime employees often resist new digital systems because they're comfortable with how things have always worked. There's also the risk that computer systems could get hacked, which would be worse than having paper backups.

stories.headline: Open-source Quantum Computing Operating System launched for diverse architectures
stories.summary: WuYue has released QCOS, an open-source, general-purpose Quantum Computing Operating System designed to provide unified software support across different quantum computer architectures including SuperConducting, Neutral Atom, and Ion Trap systems. The platform aims to standardize development and operation across varied quantum hardware.
stories.context: Quantum computers represent a completely new type of computing that doesn't use regular computer chips but instead harnesses the strange properties of quantum physics—specifically, the ability of tiny particles to exist in multiple states at once, which allows quantum computers to solve certain complex problems much faster than traditional computers. The challenge is that there are several completely different ways to build quantum computers: some use superconducting materials that must be kept extremely cold, others use neutral atoms held in place with light beams, and still others use charged atoms (ions) trapped by electromagnetic fields. The problem has been that software written for one type of quantum computer won't work on another—like having apps that only run on iPhones but not Android phones. QCOS, which stands for Quantum Computing Operating System, is essentially trying to be like a universal system that can work across all these different quantum computer types, allowing programmers to write code once and run it on any quantum hardware rather than learning different languages for each type.
stories.why_it_matters: Right now, each type of quantum computer needs its own special software, which makes developing them slow and complicated. This new operating system is like creating a universal language that works across all quantum computers, letting scientists share ideas and build things faster.
stories.other_side: Some researchers question whether a one-size-fits-all system can actually work well when the underlying quantum computers are so different from each other. There's also the risk that if this open-source system has bugs, it could hold back progress across the entire field instead of helping it.
